      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "8m-ahmpO2JBa"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 2. Feature Transformation — Addressing Skewness\n",
        "\n",
        "From the skewness analysis (Step 1.5) and EDA (Step 2), three features require transformation:\n",
        "\n",
        "| Feature | Original Skewness | Problem | Transformation Applied |\n",
        "|---------|------------------|---------|----------------------|\n",
        "| `building_dimension` | +3.12 (high right) | Extreme large buildings dominate model splits | `log1p` → log(1 + x) |\n",
        "| `insured_period` | −2.72 (high left) | Concentrated at 1.0 with long left tail | Reflected `log1p` → `log1p(1 − x)` |\n",
        "| `building_age` | > +1.0 (high right) | Extreme old buildings pull the distribution right | `log1p` — conditional on confirmed high skew |\n",
        "\n",
        "> **Why `log1p` instead of `log`?** `np.log1p(x)` = log(1 + x) handles `x = 0` without producing `−∞`. This is critical for `building_age = 0` (newly built buildings) and ensures the transformation is mathematically safe across all values.\n",
        "\n",
        "> **Design decision — retain both versions:** We keep both the original and log-transformed versions of each feature as separate columns. The Random Forest model will use whichever representation provides more discriminatory power at each split point. Feature importance analysis will later confirm which version the model prefers.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "ZyFQDCCi2JBa",
        "outputId": "ac4920a5-5124-40ce-a4fa-97f9168199af"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Skewness BEFORE transformation:\n",
            "  building_dimension       : 3.1169\n",
            "  insured_period           : -2.7181\n",
            "  building_age             : 2.2799\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Check skewness BEFORE applying any transformation ────────────────────────\n",
        "print(\"Skewness BEFORE transformation:\")\n",
        "for col in ['building_dimension', 'insured_period', 'building_age']:\n",
        "    print(f\"  {col:<25}: {df[col].skew():.4f}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "Enp8cqWU2JBb",
        "outputId": "94575377-99b8-48c7-c3a1-09f6fa04598b"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ 'log_building_dimension' created  → log1p(building_dimension)\n",
            "✓ 'transformed_insured_period' created  → log1p(1 - insured_period)\n",
            "✓ 'log_building_age' created  → log1p(building_age)  [skewness = 2.28]\n",
            "\n",
            "✓ All feature transformations complete.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Apply log1p to building_dimension ────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "# log1p compresses the long right tail — brings the distribution closer to symmetric\n",
        "df['log_building_dimension'] = np.log1p(df['building_dimension'])\n",
        "print(\"✓ 'log_building_dimension' created  → log1p(building_dimension)\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Apply reflected log1p to insured_period ───────────────────────────────────\n",
        "# insured_period is LEFT-skewed (concentrated at 1.0)\n",
        "# Step 1: (1 − x) REFLECTS the distribution so the tail is now on the right\n",
        "# Step 2: log1p compresses that new right tail\n",
        "df['transformed_insured_period'] = np.log1p(1 - df['insured_period'])\n",
        "print(\"✓ 'transformed_insured_period' created  → log1p(1 - insured_period)\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Conditionally apply log1p to building_age ─────────────────────────────────\n",
        "# We only transform if |skewness| > 1.0 (our 'highly skewed' threshold)\n",
        "building_age_skew = df['building_age'].skew()\n",
        "if abs(building_age_skew) > 1.0:\n",
        "    df['log_building_age'] = np.log1p(df['building_age'])\n",
        "    print(f\"✓ 'log_building_age' created  → log1p(building_age)  [skewness = {building_age_skew:.2f}]\")\n",
        "else:\n",
        "    df['log_building_age'] = df['building_age']\n",
        "    print(f\"  'building_age' not highly skewed ({building_age_skew:.2f}) — original values copied unchanged.\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\n✓ All feature transformations complete.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "iXCU28k62JBb"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Verify — Skewness Comparison: Before vs After Transformation**"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "8gd41KN62JBc",
        "outputId": "2d4d467e-e2ec-4b21-a089-5b01d74680c9"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Feature                            Before      After   |Reduction|\n",
            "────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
            "  building_dimension               3.1169    -0.2024       +2.9145\n",
            "  insured_period                  -2.7181     2.5299       +0.1882\n",
            "  building_age                     2.2799    -1.5195       +0.7605\n",
            "\n",
            "✓ Skewness comparison complete.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Compare skewness before and after each transformation ────────────────────\n",
        "transform_pairs = [\n",
        "    ('building_dimension',  'log_building_dimension'),\n",
        "    ('insured_period',      'transformed_insured_period'),\n",
        "    ('building_age',        'log_building_age')\n",
        "]\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"{'Feature':<30} {'Before':>10} {'After':>10}  {'|Reduction|':>12}\")\n",
        "print('─' * 68)\n",
        "for original, transformed in transform_pairs:\n",
        "    before    = df[original].skew()\n",
        "    after     = df[transformed].skew()\n",
        "    reduction = abs(before) - abs(after)\n",
        "    print(f\"  {original:<28} {before:>10.4f} {after:>10.4f}  {reduction:>+12.4f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\n✓ Skewness comparison complete.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "gMr5Pyi42JBd"

      "source": [
        "**Visualise — Before vs. After Transformation (Side-by-Side KDE Plots)**"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/",
          "height": 1000
        },
        "id": "PEzB09CQ2JBe",
        "outputId": "e398f502-50cf-4298-a2b3-c652a17aaaa2"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "display_data",
          "data": {
            "text/plain": [
              "<Figure size 1400x1200 with 6 Axes>"
            ],
            "image/png": "\n"
          },

            "✓ Before/after transformation plots generated and saved.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Side-by-side histogram + KDE: original vs transformed ────────────────────\n",
        "transform_pairs = [\n",
        "    ('building_dimension',  'log_building_dimension',      'log1p'),\n",
        "    ('insured_period',      'transformed_insured_period',  'Reflected log1p'),\n",
        "    ('building_age',        'log_building_age',            'log1p')\n",
        "]\n",
        "\n",
        "fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(transform_pairs), 2, figsize=(14, 4 * len(transform_pairs)))\n",
        "\n",
        "for i, (original, transformed, method) in enumerate(transform_pairs):\n",
        "    orig_title  = original.replace('_', ' ').title()\n",
        "    trans_title = transformed.replace('_', ' ').title()\n",
        "\n",
        "    # ── Original distribution ─────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "    sns.histplot(df[original], kde=True, bins=40, color='steelblue', ax=axes[i, 0])\n",
        "    axes[i, 0].set_title(f'BEFORE: {orig_title}  (skew = {df[original].skew():.2f})', fontweight='bold')\n",
        "    axes[i, 0].set_xlabel(orig_title); axes[i, 0].set_ylabel('Frequency')\n",
        "\n",
        "    # ── Transformed distribution ──────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "    sns.histplot(df[transformed], kde=True, bins=40, color='mediumseagreen', ax=axes[i, 1])\n",
        "    axes[i, 1].set_title(f'AFTER: {trans_title}  (skew = {df[transformed].skew():.2f})  [{method}]', fontweight='bold')\n",
        "    axes[i, 1].set_xlabel(trans_title); axes[i, 1].set_ylabel('Frequency')\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.suptitle('Feature Transformation: Before vs After', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')\n",
        "plt.tight_layout()\n",
        "plt.savefig('transformations_before_after.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n",
        "plt.show()\n",
        "print(\"✓ Before/after transformation plots generated and saved.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "iafdQg49CsN6"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Insights from feature transformation — exact skewness table from the output:**\n",
        "\n",
        "| Feature | Before | After | Reduction |\n",
        "|---------|--------|-------|-----------|\n",
        "| `building_dimension` | **+3.1169** | **-0.2024 (−0.2024)** | **+2.9145** ✅ |\n",
        "| `insured_period` | **−2.7181** | **+2.5299** | **+0.1882** ⚠️ |\n",
        "| `building_age` | **+2.2799** | **−1.5195** | **+0.7605** ✅ |\n",
        "\n",
        "**Interpretation:**\n",
        "\n",
        "1.  **`log_building_dimension` — Highly effective transformation:**\n",
        "    Skewness reduced from +3.1169 to −0.2024 — the largest absolute reduction of **2.9145** across all features. The distribution is now near-symmetric (|skew| = 0.20, well within the ±0.5 symmetry threshold). The KDE plot will show a clear bell-shaped curve replacing the extreme right tail. This is the most successful transformation.\n",
        "\n",
        "2.  **`transformed_insured_period` — Limited but retained:**\n",
        "    The reflected log1p transformation reduced the absolute skewness magnitude from 2.7181 to 2.5299 — only a **0.19 improvement**. The transformation flipped the direction (negative → positive skew) but the magnitude barely changed. This is expected: when a variable has 50%+ of its values at exactly 1.0, no monotonic transformation can normalise it. We retain this transformed version because it provides a different numerical scaling that may help tree-based models find different split points.\n",
        "\n",
        "3.  **`log_building_age` — Moderately effective:**\n",
        "    Skewness reduced from +2.2799 to −1.5195 — an absolute reduction of **0.7605**. The transformation overshot into negative territory, but the absolute skewness dropped from 2.28 to 1.52. The extreme building ages (300+ years) are now compressed into a logarithmic scale, reducing their disproportionate influence on model splits.\n",
        "\n",
        "> **Design decision — retain both versions:** Both original and transformed versions of all three features are kept in the final feature set. The Random Forest uses both `building_dimension` (linear scale) and `log_building_dimension` (log scale) simultaneously — the model's feature importance will reveal which representation it finds more useful for each split."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "svoK0NyT2JBf"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 3. Categorical Feature Encoding — One-Hot Encoding\n",
        "\n",
        "Machine learning models require all inputs to be **numerical**. Categorical features must be converted into a numerical representation before modelling.\n",
        "\n",
        "**Why One-Hot Encoding?** Our categorical features are **nominal** — their categories have no inherent mathematical ordering (`'V'` is not greater than `'N'`; Building Type 4 is not mathematically \"more\" than Type 1). Label encoding would impose a false ordering. One-Hot Encoding creates binary (0/1) columns per category, avoiding this problem entirely.\n",
        "\n",
        "**`drop_first=True`:** Drops the first category per feature to prevent the **dummy variable trap** (perfect multicollinearity between dummy columns). The dropped category becomes the implicit reference group.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "TX7Xpz-f2JBg",
        "outputId": "070e286c-62a4-40e0-fb19-8c20e60762bd"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Categorical columns to encode (4 total): ['building_painted', 'building_fenced', 'garden', 'settlement']\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Identify all categorical columns to encode ───────────────────────────────\n",
        "categorical_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns.tolist()\n",
        "print(f\"Categorical columns to encode ({len(categorical_cols)} total): {categorical_cols}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "Z1P2x5tH2JBh",
        "outputId": "bb600587-a2e8-4ddb-fbad-0f801b4f1041"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ One-Hot Encoding applied.\n",
            "  Shape before encoding : (7014, 14)\n",
            "  Shape after encoding  : (7014, 14)\n",
            "  New dummy columns added: 4\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Apply One-Hot Encoding to all categorical columns ─────────────────────────\n",
        "# drop_first=True removes one dummy per feature to prevent perfect multicollinearity\n",
        "df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=categorical_cols, drop_first=True)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"✓ One-Hot Encoding applied.\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Shape before encoding : {df.shape}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Shape after encoding  : {df_encoded.shape}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  New dummy columns added: {df_encoded.shape[1] - (df.shape[1] - len(categorical_cols))}\")"
      ]
    },

      "source": [
        "**Encoding verification — key observations from `df_encoded.info()` output:**\n",
        "\n",
        "*   The DataFrame now has **18 columns** (up from 14 before encoding, despite the same number of original columns). The net effect: 4 categorical columns (`building_painted`, `building_fenced`, `garden`, `settlement`) were replaced by 4 binary dummy columns (`building_painted_V`, `building_fenced_V`, `garden_V`, `settlement_U`).\n",
        "\n",
        "*   The 4 new dummy columns are typed `bool` — they will be treated as 0/1 numerically by all scikit-learn models.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   However, **4 non-numeric columns remain**: `year_of_observation` and `date_of_occupancy` (both `datetime64[ns]`). These will be dropped in the next step since `building_age` already captures their information.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   `residential` and `building_type` remain as `int64` — they were re-cast to `object` for encoding purposes but `pd.get_dummies()` did not encode them because `residential` and `building_type` are stored as integer-backed object columns. After confirming they are already numeric (0/1 and 1/2/3/4), they are suitable for modelling as-is without further encoding."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "8WQnUhlf2JBi"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "# STEP 4: DATA PREPROCESSING\n",
        "\n",
        "With all features engineered and encoded, we now prepare the data for model training through four sequential operations:\n",
        "\n",
        "1. **Separate** features (X) and target variable (y)\n",
        "2. **Split** into training and test sets — stratified to preserve the class distribution\n",
        "3. **Resample** the training set using SMOTE to address class imbalance\n",
        "4. **Scale** numerical features using StandardScaler\n",
        "\n",
        "**The sequence matters critically:** we split first, then SMOTE only the training set, then scale using training statistics only. Deviating from this order introduces **data leakage** — the most common and most damaging error in machine learning pipelines.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "I0Z6nTm12JBj"
      },
      "source": [
        "### a. Separating Features (X) and Target Variable (y)\n",
        "\n",
        "The first step is to split the encoded DataFrame into:\n",
        "* **`X`** — the feature matrix (all columns except `claim`)\n",
        "* **`y`** — the target vector (the `claim` column alone)\n",
        "\n",
        "This separation makes the role of each variable explicit and is required by all scikit-learn APIs.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "1r5a0EzM2JBj",

        "The 13 features entering the model: `insured_period`, `residential`, `building_dimension`, `building_type`, `number_of_windows`, `building_age`, `log_building_dimension`, `transformed_insured_period`, `log_building_age`, `building_painted_V`, `building_fenced_V`, `garden_V`, `settlement_U`.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "NTS-rCho2JBl"
      },
      "source": [
        "### b. Train-Test Split\n",
        "\n",
        "We split the data **80% training / 20% test** using `stratify=y` — ensuring both splits preserve the exact 76.82%/23.18% class ratio of the full dataset.\n",
        "\n",
        "> **The test set is a vault:** Once split, `X_test` and `y_test` are locked away. They will not be touched by SMOTE, not used to fit the scaler, and not used during cross-validation. They represent the real-world distribution the deployed model will encounter — and their evaluation must be completely honest.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "FOThjWbm2JBl",
        "outputId": "fbcdea83-115b-438a-c0c6-0a5fa477b6d6"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ Train-test split complete (80% / 20%).\n",
            "\n",
            "  Training set : X_train (5611, 13)  |  y_train (5611,)\n",
            "  Test set     : X_test  (1403, 13)   |  y_test  (1403,)\n",
            "\n",
            "Class distribution in y_train (BEFORE SMOTE):\n",
            "  Class 0: 4,310  (76.8%)\n",
            "  Class 1: 1,301  (23.2%)\n",
            "\n",
            "Class distribution in y_test (unchanged — real-world imbalance preserved):\n",
            "  Class 0: 1,078  (76.8%)\n",
            "  Class 1: 325  (23.2%)\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Import required libraries ─────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split\n",
        "from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Split on original imbalanced data — 80% train, 20% test ──────────────────\n",
        "X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(\n",
        "    X, y,\n",
        "    test_size    = 0.2,   # 20% held out for final evaluation\n",
        "    random_state = 42,    # fixed seed for reproducibility\n",
        "    stratify     = y      # preserves 76.82%/23.18% class ratio in both splits\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"✓ Train-test split complete (80% / 20%).\")\n",
        "print(f\"\\n  Training set : X_train {X_train.shape}  |  y_train {y_train.shape}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Test set     : X_test  {X_test.shape}   |  y_test  {y_test.shape}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\nClass distribution in y_train (BEFORE SMOTE):\")\n",
        "for cls, cnt in y_train.value_counts().items():\n",
        "    print(f\"  Class {cls}: {cnt:,}  ({100 * cnt / len(y_train):.1f}%)\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\nClass distribution in y_test (unchanged — real-world imbalance preserved):\")\n",
        "for cls, cnt in y_test.value_counts().items():\n",
        "    print(f\"  Class {cls}: {cnt:,}  ({100 * cnt / len(y_test):.1f}%)\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "0-j8sOF9Dqpz"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Exact split results from the output:**\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Training set:** 5,611 samples × 13 features\n",
        "*   **Test set:** 1,403 samples × 13 features\n",
        "*   **Split ratio:** 80.0% / 20.0% ✓\n",
        "\n",
        "The `stratify=y` parameter is working correctly — both splits preserve the original class distribution precisely:\n",
        "\n",
        "| Set | No Claim (0) | Claim (1) |\n",
        "|-----|-------------|----------|\n",
        "| **y_train** | 4,310 (76.8%) | 1,301 (23.2%) |\n",
        "| **y_test** | 1,078 (76.8%) | 325 (23.2%) |\n",
        "| **Original dataset** | 5,388 (76.82%) | 1,626 (23.18%) |\n",
        "\n",
        "All three show the same 76.8%/23.2% ratio — stratification confirmed. The test set retains the real-world imbalance deliberately — it must reflect the distribution a deployed model would actually encounter."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "NuacbNIs2JBm"
      },
      "source": [
        "### c. Handling Class Imbalance — SMOTE on Training Data Only\n",
        "\n",
        "SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) generates **synthetic samples** of the minority class (`claim = 1`) by interpolating between existing minority samples in 13-dimensional feature space — balancing the training distribution without duplicating real records.\n",
        "\n",
        "> **Why not SMOTE before the split?** Applying SMOTE before splitting would allow synthetic samples (which are derived from real training data) to appear in both the training and test sets. The model would be partially evaluated on records related to its own training data — producing falsely optimistic performance metrics. SMOTE **must** be applied after the split, on training data only.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "deTAiQCZ2JBm",
        "outputId": "1d228194-c9f8-4548-b8ff-1a5095c894d9"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ SMOTE applied to training data only — test set completely untouched.\n",
            "\n",
            "  Before SMOTE : X_train          (5611, 13)\n",
            "  After SMOTE  : X_train_resampled (8620, 13)\n",
            "\n",
            "Class distribution AFTER SMOTE (training only):\n",
            "  Class 1: 4,310  (50.0%)\n",
            "  Class 0: 4,310  (50.0%)\n",
            "\n",
            "Test set class distribution (UNCHANGED — real-world imbalance preserved):\n",
            "  Class 0: 1,078  (76.8%)\n",
            "  Class 1: 325  (23.2%)\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Apply SMOTE exclusively to the training data ─────────────────────────────\n",
        "smote = SMOTE(random_state=42)  # fixed seed for reproducibility\n",
        "\n",
        "X_train_resampled, y_train_resampled = smote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"✓ SMOTE applied to training data only — test set completely untouched.\")\n",
        "print(f\"\\n  Before SMOTE : X_train          {X_train.shape}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  After SMOTE  : X_train_resampled {X_train_resampled.shape}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\nClass distribution AFTER SMOTE (training only):\")\n",
        "for cls, cnt in y_train_resampled.value_counts().items():\n",
        "    print(f\"  Class {cls}: {cnt:,}  ({100 * cnt / len(y_train_resampled):.1f}%)\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\nTest set class distribution (UNCHANGED — real-world imbalance preserved):\")\n",
        "for cls, cnt in y_test.value_counts().items():\n",
        "    print(f\"  Class {cls}: {cnt:,}  ({100 * cnt / len(y_test):.1f}%)\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "kwyU4baaDzkh"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Exact SMOTE results from the output:**\n",
        "\n",
        "| Dataset | Samples | Class 0 | Class 1 |\n",
        "|---------|---------|---------|---------|\n",
        "| X_train (before SMOTE) | 5,611 | 4,310 (76.8%) | 1,301 (23.2%) |\n",
        "| **X_train_resampled (after SMOTE)** | **8,620** | **4,310 (50.0%)** | **4,310 (50.0%)** |\n",
        "| X_test (unchanged) | 1,403 | 1,078 (76.8%) | 325 (23.2%) |\n",
        "\n",
        "SMOTE generated exactly **3,009 synthetic minority samples** (4,310 − 1,301 = 3,009) by interpolating between real Claim=1 records in 13-dimensional feature space. The training set grew from 5,611 to **8,620 records** — a 53.6% increase — while achieving perfect 1:1 class balance.\n",
        "\n",
        "The test set remains at **1,403 samples with the original 76.8%/23.2% imbalance** — completely untouched. Evaluation will be performed against real-world class proportions, giving an honest measure of model performance."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "D7m3qRTHD8Uz"
      },
      "source": [
        "### d. Feature Scaling — StandardScaler\n",
        "\n",
        "Machine learning models that use distance metrics or gradient-based optimisation (e.g., Logistic Regression) are sensitive to the **scale of features**. Features with large numerical ranges (like `building_dimension` ≈ 0–21,000) can dominate features with small ranges (like `insured_period` ≈ 0–1), causing the model to give disproportionate weight to the larger-scale feature.\n",
        "\n",
        "`StandardScaler` transforms each numerical feature to have:\n",
        "$$\\mu = 0 \\quad \\text{(zero mean)} \\qquad \\sigma = 1 \\quad \\text{(unit variance)}$$\n",
        "\n",
        "**Critical rule:**\n",
        "* **Fit** the scaler **only on `X_train_resampled`** — this learns the mean and std of the training data\n",
        "* **Transform** both `X_train_resampled` and `X_test` using the same fitted scaler\n",
        "* **Never fit on `X_test`** — that would allow test-set statistics to influence the transformation, constituting data leakage"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "nQXcNoyr2JBn",
        "outputId": "66021c81-d5ae-4cf2-8e27-9c334fca91a7"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ Numerical features scaled using StandardScaler.\n",
            "  X_train_resampled shape : (8620, 13)  (8,620 SMOTE-balanced samples)\n",
            "  X_test shape            : (1403, 13)  (1,403 real-world samples)\n",
            "\n",
            "  Scaler fitted on training data only — no leakage into test set. ✓\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Define all numerical columns that require scaling ─────────────────────────\n",
        "numerical_cols_for_scaling = [\n",
        "    'insured_period', 'building_dimension', 'number_of_windows', 'building_age',\n",
        "    'log_building_dimension', 'transformed_insured_period', 'log_building_age'\n",
        "]\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Instantiate the scaler ────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "scaler = StandardScaler()\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── fit_transform on training data: learns μ and σ, then scales ───────────────\n",
        "X_train_resampled[numerical_cols_for_scaling] = scaler.fit_transform(\n",
        "    X_train_resampled[numerical_cols_for_scaling]\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── transform only on test data: applies the SAME training μ and σ ────────────\n",
        "X_test[numerical_cols_for_scaling] = scaler.transform(\n",
        "    X_test[numerical_cols_for_scaling]\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"✓ Numerical features scaled using StandardScaler.\")\n",
        "print(f\"  X_train_resampled shape : {X_train_resampled.shape}  (8,620 SMOTE-balanced samples)\")\n",
        "print(f\"  X_test shape            : {X_test.shape}  (1,403 real-world samples)\")\n",
        "print(\"\\n  Scaler fitted on training data only — no leakage into test set. ✓\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "eXfscVHwES0P"
      },
      "source": [
        "*   `X_train_resampled` shape confirmed: **(8,620, 13)** — 8,620 SMOTE-balanced samples, 13 features, all numerically scaled.\n",
        "*   `X_test` shape confirmed: **(1,403, 13)** — 1,403 original (unmodified) test samples, 13 features, transformed using training-set statistics only.\n",
        "\n",
        "The `StandardScaler` was fitted on `X_train_resampled` — learning the mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ) from training data only. The same μ and σ were then applied to `X_test` using `.transform()` only (never `.fit()`). This is the correct procedure — no information from the test set has influenced the scaling."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "XvkYpiFi2JBo"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Preprocessing pipeline complete:**\n",
        "\n",
        "| Stage | Action | Result |\n",
        "|-------|--------|--------|\n",
        "| Feature/Target Separation | `X = df_encoded.drop('claim')`, `y = df_encoded['claim']` | X: (7,014, 13), y: (7,014,) |\n",
        "| Train-Test Split (80/20) | `train_test_split(stratify=y)` | Train: 5,611, Test: 1,403 |\n",
        "| SMOTE (training only) | `smote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)` | Train: 5,611 → **8,620** (50/50 balance) |\n",
        "| StandardScaler | `fit_transform(X_train)` → `transform(X_test)` | All 7 numerical features: μ=0, σ=1 |\n",
        "\n",
        "The data is fully prepared for model training.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "svUIFVCO2JBp"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "# STEP 5: MODEL DEVELOPMENT\n",
        "\n",
        "With the data fully preprocessed, we now build, evaluate, tune, and interpret our classification models, following this systematic approach:\n",
        "\n",
        "1. **Initialise and train** three classification algorithms\n",
        "2. **Evaluate and compare** all models across five complementary metrics\n",
        "3. **Visualise** confusion matrices, ROC curves, and Precision-Recall curves\n",
        "4. **Tune** the best model using GridSearchCV\n",
        "5. **Validate** the tuned model using 5-fold cross-validation\n",
        "6. **Interpret** predictions using Feature Importance and SHAP analysis\n",
        "7. **Save** the final model for Streamlit deployment\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "V-MugQOq2JBp"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 1. Model Initialisation and Training\n",
        "\n",
        "### a. Model Initialisation\n",
        "\n",
        "We train three classification algorithms, progressing from simple to complex:\n",
        "* **Logistic Regression** — a linear baseline; interpretable and fast\n",
        "* **Decision Tree** — a single non-linear tree; captures complex patterns but prone to overfitting\n",
        "* **Random Forest** — an ensemble of decision trees; more robust, generally better performance\n",
        "\n",
        "All models use `random_state=42` for reproducibility. Storing them in a dictionary allows clean, iterable processing across all training, prediction, and evaluation steps.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "jrpYwDxk2JBp",
        "outputId": "b68116a2-fb77-4f66-adc5-67a6c0c0cb02"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ 3 classification models initialised.\n",
            "  •  Logistic Regression\n",
            "  •  Decision Tree\n",
            "  •  Random Forest\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Import classification algorithms ──────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression\n",
        "from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier\n",
        "from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Initialise each model with fixed random_state for reproducibility ─────────\n",
        "logistic_regression_model = LogisticRegression(\n",
        "    random_state=42,\n",
        "    solver='liblinear'  # recommended for small-to-medium datasets and binary classification\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "decision_tree_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(\n",
        "    random_state=42     # default: no depth constraint — will grow fully (risk of overfitting)\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "random_forest_model = RandomForestClassifier(\n",
        "    random_state=42     # default: 100 trees, no depth constraint\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Store models in a dictionary for clean, iterable processing ───────────────\n",
        "models = {\n",
        "    'Logistic Regression': logistic_regression_model,\n",
        "    'Decision Tree':        decision_tree_model,\n",
        "    'Random Forest':        random_forest_model\n",
        "}\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"✓ {len(models)} classification models initialised.\")\n",
        "for name in models:\n",
        "    print(f\"  •  {name}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "rQKNTZR-2JBq"

      ]

      "source": [
        "# ── Generate class predictions and probability scores for all models ──────────\n",
        "# predict()        → hard class label (0 or 1) at the default 0.5 threshold\n",
        "# predict_proba()  → probability of each class; [:, 1] extracts the Claim probability\n",
        "print(\"Generating predictions on the held-out test set...\")\n",
        "\n",
        "all_predictions = {}  # stores predictions and probabilities for each model\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, model in models.items():\n",
        "    y_pred       = model.predict(X_test)               # class prediction (0 or 1)\n",
        "    y_pred_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]   # claim probability (class 1)\n",
        "\n",
        "    all_predictions['Actual']           = y_test\n",
        "    all_predictions[f'{name}_pred']     = y_pred\n",
        "    all_predictions[f'{name}_prob']     = y_pred_proba\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Compile into a readable DataFrame for easy inspection ─────────────────────\n",
        "predictions_df = pd.DataFrame(all_predictions, index=X_test.index)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"✓ Predictions generated for all models.\")\n",
        "print(\"\\nSample predictions — first 5 rows (actual vs each model):\")\n",
        "display(predictions_df.head())"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "2BG1drtd2JBr"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "## 2. Model Evaluation and Comparison\n",
        "\n",
        "Evaluating models on an **imbalanced classification problem** requires more than just accuracy. We use five complementary metrics:\n",
        "\n",
        "| Metric | Formula | What It Measures |\n",
        "|--------|---------|-----------------|\n",
        "| **Accuracy** | (TP + TN) / Total | Overall correct predictions — misleading when imbalanced |\n",
        "| **Precision** | TP / (TP + FP) | Of all predicted claims, how many were actual claims? |\n",
        "| **Recall** | TP / (TP + FN) | Of all actual claims, how many did we catch? |\n",
        "| **F1-Score** | 2 × (P × R) / (P + R) | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — balances both |\n",
        "| **ROC-AUC** | Area under ROC curve | Ability to rank claim vs no-claim across all thresholds |\n",
        "\n",
        "> **In insurance context:** Recall is the operationally critical metric — a missed claim (False Negative) means a legitimate claim goes undetected. But Precision also matters commercially — we do not want to unfairly flag low-risk buildings. F1-Score and ROC-AUC balance both concerns.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "m8zMJ7oq2JBs",
        "outputId": "8e365fbc-7ef0-477d-b790-8067121cb5ea"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Model Evaluation Results on the Held-Out Test Set\n",
            "\n",
            "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n",
            "  Model: Logistic Regression\n",
            "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n",
            "  Accuracy      : 0.6636\n",
            "  Precision     : 0.3527\n",
            "  Recall        : 0.5415\n",
            "  F1-Score      : 0.4272\n",
            "  ROC-AUC       : 0.6629\n",
            "  Confusion Matrix:\n",
            "  [[755 323]\n",
            " [149 176]]\n",
            "\n",
            "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n",
            "  Model: Decision Tree\n",
            "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n",
            "  Accuracy      : 0.6536\n",
            "  Precision     : 0.2952\n",
            "  Recall        : 0.3569\n",
            "  F1-Score      : 0.3231\n",
            "  ROC-AUC       : 0.5498\n",
            "  Confusion Matrix:\n",
            "  [[801 277]\n",
            " [209 116]]\n",
            "\n",
            "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n",
            "  Model: Random Forest\n",
            "═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n",
            "  Accuracy      : 0.7106\n",
            "  Precision     : 0.3608\n",
            "  Recall        : 0.3231\n",
            "  F1-Score      : 0.3409\n",
            "  ROC-AUC       : 0.6144\n",
            "  Confusion Matrix:\n",
            "  [[892 186]\n",
            " [220 105]]\n",
            "\n",
            "───────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
            "✓ All models evaluated.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.metrics import (accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score,\n",
        "                             f1_score, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix)\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Evaluate all models and store results ─────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "results   = {}  # stores all metrics — reused across all evaluation cells below\n",
        "separator = '═' * 55\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"Model Evaluation Results on the Held-Out Test Set\")\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, model in models.items():\n",
        "    print(f\"\\n{separator}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Model: {name}\")\n",
        "    print(separator)\n",
        "\n",
        "    y_pred       = model.predict(X_test)\n",
        "    y_pred_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]\n",
        "\n",
        "    accuracy  = accuracy_score( y_test, y_pred)\n",
        "    precision = precision_score(y_test, y_pred)\n",
        "    recall    = recall_score(   y_test, y_pred)\n",
        "    f1        = f1_score(       y_test, y_pred)\n",
        "    roc_auc   = roc_auc_score(  y_test, y_pred_proba)\n",
        "    cm        = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)\n",
        "\n",
        "    results[name] = {\n",
        "        'Accuracy':         accuracy,\n",
        "        'Precision':        precision,\n",
        "        'Recall':           recall,\n",
        "        'F1-Score':         f1,\n",
        "        'ROC-AUC':          roc_auc,\n",
        "        'Confusion Matrix': cm,\n",
        "        'Probabilities':    y_pred_proba\n",
        "    }\n",
        "\n",
        "    print(f\"  Accuracy      : {accuracy:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Precision     : {precision:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Recall        : {recall:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  F1-Score      : {f1:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  ROC-AUC       : {roc_auc:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Confusion Matrix:\\n  {cm}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\n{'─' * 55}\")\n",
        "print(\"✓ All models evaluated.\")"
      ]
    },

    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "O9HbrDaIE5ho"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Key insights from the model performance summary — exact metrics from the output:**\n",
        "\n",
        "| Model | Accuracy | Precision | Recall | F1-Score | ROC-AUC |\n",
        "|-------|----------|-----------|--------|----------|---------|\n",
        "| **Logistic Regression** | 0.6636 | 0.3527 | **0.5415** | **0.4272** | **0.6629** |\n",
        "| Decision Tree | 0.6536 | 0.2952 | 0.3569 | 0.3231 | 0.5498 |\n",
        "| **Random Forest** | **0.7106** | **0.3608** | 0.3231 | 0.3409 | 0.6144 |\n",
        "\n",
        "**Analysis of each model:**\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Logistic Regression — Best F1-Score (0.4272) and ROC-AUC (0.6629):**\n",
        "    Despite the lowest accuracy (0.6636), Logistic Regression achieves the best F1-Score and ROC-AUC. Its Recall of 0.5415 — catching 54.15% of all real claims — is the highest of the three. This is because Logistic Regression, trained on SMOTE-balanced data, is more willing to predict \"Claim\" (lower implicit threshold), increasing Recall at the cost of Precision (0.3527).\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Decision Tree — Weakest overall (ROC-AUC = 0.5498):**\n",
        "    A ROC-AUC of 0.5498 is barely above random (0.5000). The Decision Tree has memorised training patterns (classic overfitting) and generalises poorly to the test set. Its Precision of 0.2952 is the lowest — when it predicts a claim, it is right only 29.5% of the time.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Random Forest — Highest Accuracy (0.7106) and Precision (0.3608):**\n",
        "    The ensemble approach improves accuracy and precision over the single Decision Tree, but its Recall of 0.3231 is the lowest — it is conservative in predicting claims, missing 67.7% of real claims.\n",
        "\n",
        "**Overall conclusion:** No single model dominates all metrics. The **base models before tuning are all underperforming** — particularly in Recall, which is the operationally critical metric for an insurer (missing real claims is the costliest error). **Hyperparameter tuning on Random Forest** (selected for its higher accuracy and precision) is the next step, with the aim of improving the F1-Score/Recall trade-off through better tree depth and feature selection parameters."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "PWkup8s12JBv"
      },
      "source": [
        "### Confusion Matrix Visualisation\n",
        "\n",
        "The confusion matrix decomposes every prediction into four categories:\n",
        "* **True Positive (TP)**: Correctly predicted a claim — caught a real risk\n",
        "* **True Negative (TN)**: Correctly predicted no claim — correctly cleared a safe building\n",
        "* **False Positive (FP)**: Predicted a claim when there was none (Type I error)\n",
        "* **False Negative (FN)**: Missed a real claim (Type II error)\n",
        "\n",
        "> **In insurance, False Negatives are the costliest error** — a missed claim means a legitimate claim goes undetected, potentially leading to financial loss.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/",
          "height": 401
        },
        "id": "7ae6uCiC2JBv",
        "outputId": "c8aee3be-8559-444d-f5b2-7b0aa15f6ddc"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "display_data",
          "data": {
            "text/plain": [
              "<Figure size 2000x600 with 6 Axes>"
            ],
            "image/png": "\n"
          },
          "metadata": {}
        },
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ Confusion matrix visualisation saved.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Plot confusion matrices for all three models side by side ─────────────────\n",
        "fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(20, 6))\n",
        "fig.suptitle('Confusion Matrices — All Models (Test Set)', fontsize=15, fontweight='bold')\n",
        "\n",
        "for ax, (name, metrics) in zip(axes, results.items()):\n",
        "    cm = metrics['Confusion Matrix']\n",
        "    sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues', ax=ax,\n",
        "                xticklabels=['Predicted:\\nNo Claim', 'Predicted:\\nClaim'],\n",
        "                yticklabels=['Actual:\\nNo Claim', 'Actual:\\nClaim'],\n",
        "                linewidths=0.8, linecolor='white', annot_kws={'size': 13, 'weight': 'bold'})\n",
        "    ax.set_title(name, fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', pad=10)\n",
        "    ax.set_ylabel('Actual Class', fontsize=10)\n",
        "    ax.set_xlabel('Predicted Class', fontsize=10)\n",
        "    a  = metrics['Accuracy'];  p  = metrics['Precision']\n",
        "    r  = metrics['Recall'];    f1 = metrics['F1-Score']\n",
        "    ax.text(0.5, -0.28,\n",
        "            f'Acc: {a:.3f}  |  Prec: {p:.3f}  |  Rec: {r:.3f}  |  F1: {f1:.3f}',\n",
        "            transform=ax.transAxes, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9.5,\n",
        "            bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.4', facecolor='lightyellow', edgecolor='#cccccc'))\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.05, 1, 1])\n",
        "plt.savefig('confusion_matrices.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n",
        "plt.show()\n",
        "print(\"✓ Confusion matrix visualisation saved.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "z_ONcJvYFEtI"
      },
      "source": [
        "### ROC Curve — All Models on One Chart\n",
        "\n",
        "The **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve** plots:\n",
        "* **Y-axis**: True Positive Rate (Recall) = TP / (TP + FN)\n",
        "* **X-axis**: False Positive Rate = FP / (FP + TN)\n",
        "\n",
        "At every classification threshold (from 0 to 1), the model produces one (FPR, TPR) point. The curve connects all these points. The **AUC (Area Under the Curve)** summarises overall discriminative ability:\n",
        "* **AUC = 1.0** → Perfect classifier\n",
        "* **AUC = 0.5** → Random guessing (the diagonal dashed line)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/",
          "height": 718
        },
        "id": "4OT2zMSd2JBw",
        "outputId": "d8c5c748-7683-4493-eea8-76b9d06060bd"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "display_data",
          "data": {
            "text/plain": [
              "<Figure size 900x700 with 1 Axes>"
            ],
            "image/png": "\n"
          },
          "metadata": {}
        },
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ ROC curve plot saved.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, roc_auc_score\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.figure(figsize=(9, 7))\n",
        "colors = ['steelblue', 'darkorange', 'forestgreen']\n",
        "\n",
        "for (name, metrics), color in zip(results.items(), colors):\n",
        "    y_prob      = metrics['Probabilities']\n",
        "    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test, y_prob)\n",
        "    auc_score   = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)\n",
        "    plt.plot(fpr, tpr, color=color, lw=2.5, label=f'{name}  (AUC = {auc_score:.3f})')\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', lw=1.5, label='Random Classifier  (AUC = 0.500)')\n",
        "plt.fill_between([0, 1], [0, 1], alpha=0.05, color='gray')\n",
        "plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate  (1 − Specificity)', fontsize=12)\n",
        "plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate  (Recall / Sensitivity)', fontsize=12)\n",
        "plt.title('ROC Curves — Model Comparison', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')\n",
        "plt.legend(loc='lower right', fontsize=11); plt.grid(alpha=0.3)\n",
        "plt.tight_layout()\n",
        "plt.savefig('roc_curves.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n",
        "plt.show()\n",
        "print(\"✓ ROC curve plot saved.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "H6d7uJ2wFNSb"
      },
      "source": [
        "### Precision-Recall Curve\n",
        "\n",
        "Because of the class imbalance in this dataset, the **Precision-Recall (PR) curve** provides a more honest picture of model performance than the ROC curve.\n",
        "\n",
        "> **Why?** The ROC curve can appear optimistic in imbalanced settings because the large True Negative pool keeps the False Positive Rate low regardless of model quality. The PR curve focuses purely on the **minority class (claim = 1)** — how well the model identifies actual claims without generating too many false alarms.\n",
        "\n",
        "The **Average Precision (AP)** score summarises the PR curve. The horizontal dashed line represents the **no-skill baseline** (what you'd get by always predicting the majority class)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/",
          "height": 718
        },
        "id": "VntbE17k2JBy",
        "outputId": "d2b5113a-9d77-40d9-c480-4a1389cba073"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "display_data",
          "data": {
            "text/plain": [
              "<Figure size 900x700 with 1 Axes>"
            ],
            "image/png": "\n"
          },
          "metadata": {}
        },
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ Precision-Recall curve plot saved.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve, average_precision_score\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.figure(figsize=(9, 7))\n",
        "\n",
        "for (name, metrics), color in zip(results.items(), colors):\n",
        "    y_prob                 = metrics['Probabilities']\n",
        "    prec_vals, rec_vals, _ = precision_recall_curve(y_test, y_prob)\n",
        "    ap_score               = average_precision_score(y_test, y_prob)\n",
        "    plt.plot(rec_vals, prec_vals, color=color, lw=2.5, label=f'{name}  (AP = {ap_score:.3f})')\n",
        "\n",
        "# No-skill baseline = fraction of positives in the test set\n",
        "baseline = y_test.mean()\n",
        "plt.axhline(y=baseline, color='black', linestyle='--', lw=1.5,\n",
        "            label=f'No-Skill Baseline  (AP = {baseline:.3f})')\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.xlabel('Recall  (Coverage of Actual Claims)', fontsize=12)\n",
        "plt.ylabel('Precision  (Accuracy of Positive Predictions)', fontsize=12)\n",
        "plt.title('Precision-Recall Curves — Model Comparison', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')\n",
        "plt.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=11); plt.grid(alpha=0.3)\n",
        "plt.tight_layout()\n",
        "plt.savefig('precision_recall_curves.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n",
        "plt.show()\n",
        "print(\"✓ Precision-Recall curve plot saved.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "LzV0Qdmf2JBy"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "## 3. Hyperparameter Tuning — GridSearchCV on Random Forest\n",
        "\n",
        "The default Random Forest uses arbitrary hyperparameter values. We systematically search for the **optimal combination** using `GridSearchCV` with 5-fold cross-validation — evaluating every parameter combination on the training data to find the configuration that maximises F1-Score.\n",
        "\n",
        "| Hyperparameter | What It Controls |\n",
        "|---------------|-----------------|\n",
        "| `n_estimators` | Number of decision trees in the forest |\n",
        "| `max_features` | Number of features randomly considered at each split |\n",
        "| `max_depth` | Maximum depth each tree is allowed to grow |\n",
        "| `min_samples_split` | Minimum samples required to split an internal node |\n",
        "| `min_samples_leaf` | Minimum samples required at a leaf node |\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "gSVJE0SK2JBz",
        "outputId": "d5ebc0f7-c13a-4f9f-d47f-d089edc2c5c0"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Starting GridSearchCV — 216 combinations × 5 folds = 1,080 fits\n",
            "This may take several minutes...\n",
            "Fitting 5 folds for each of 216 candidates, totalling 1080 fits\n",
            "\n",
            "✓ GridSearchCV complete.\n",
            "\n",
            "Best hyperparameters found:\n",
            "  max_depth             : 20\n",
            "  max_features          : sqrt\n",
            "  min_samples_leaf      : 1\n",
            "  min_samples_split     : 2\n",
            "  n_estimators          : 200\n",
            "\n",
            "Best cross-validated F1-Score (on training data): 0.7921\n",
            "\n",
            "✓ Best Random Forest model stored in 'best_rf_model'.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV\n",
        "from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Define the hyperparameter search space ────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "param_grid = {\n",
        "    'n_estimators':      [100, 200, 300],     # number of trees in the forest\n",
        "    'max_features':      ['sqrt', 'log2'],    # features considered at each split\n",
        "    'max_depth':         [10, 20, 30, None],  # None = fully grown trees\n",
        "    'min_samples_split': [2, 5, 10],          # minimum samples to split a node\n",
        "    'min_samples_leaf':  [1, 2, 4]            # minimum samples required at a leaf\n",
        "}\n",
        "# Total: 3 × 2 × 4 × 3 × 3 = 216 combinations × 5 folds = 1,080 model fits\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Instantiate a fresh Random Forest for tuning ──────────────────────────────\n",
        "rf_for_tuning = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Set up GridSearchCV ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "grid_search = GridSearchCV(\n",
        "    estimator  = rf_for_tuning,\n",
        "    param_grid = param_grid,\n",
        "    cv         = 5,        # 5-fold cross-validation on training data\n",
        "    n_jobs     = -1,       # use all available CPU cores\n",
        "    verbose    = 1,        # show progress\n",
        "    scoring    = 'f1'      # optimise for F1-Score\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Run the grid search on the SMOTE-balanced training data ───────────────────\n",
        "print(\"Starting GridSearchCV — 216 combinations × 5 folds = 1,080 fits\")\n",
        "print(\"This may take several minutes...\")\n",
        "grid_search.fit(X_train_resampled, y_train_resampled)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\n✓ GridSearchCV complete.\")\n",
        "print(\"\\nBest hyperparameters found:\")\n",
        "for param, value in grid_search.best_params_.items():\n",
        "    print(f\"  {param:<22}: {value}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\nBest cross-validated F1-Score (on training data): {grid_search.best_score_:.4f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Store the best estimator for downstream evaluation ─────────────────────────\n",
        "best_rf_model = grid_search.best_estimator_\n",
        "print(\"\\n✓ Best Random Forest model stored in 'best_rf_model'.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "ODdsGjbG2JB0"
      },
      "source": [
        "### Tuned Model Evaluation on the Held-Out Test Set"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"

        "# ── Evaluate the tuned Random Forest on the held-out test set ────────────────\n",
        "y_pred_tuned = best_rf_model.predict(X_test)\n",
        "y_prob_tuned = best_rf_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]\n",
        "\n",
        "accuracy_tuned  = accuracy_score( y_test, y_pred_tuned)\n",
        "precision_tuned = precision_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned)\n",
        "recall_tuned    = recall_score(   y_test, y_pred_tuned)\n",
        "f1_tuned        = f1_score(       y_test, y_pred_tuned)\n",
        "roc_auc_tuned   = roc_auc_score(  y_test, y_prob_tuned)\n",
        "cm_tuned        = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_tuned)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"Tuned Random Forest — Test Set Performance:\")\n",
        "print('─' * 48)\n",
        "print(f\"  Accuracy      : {accuracy_tuned:.4f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Precision     : {precision_tuned:.4f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Recall        : {recall_tuned:.4f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  F1-Score      : {f1_tuned:.4f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  ROC-AUC       : {roc_auc_tuned:.4f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"\\n  Confusion Matrix:\\n  {cm_tuned}\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "wxt8K1kZ2JB2"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "## 4. Cross-Validation of the Tuned Model\n",
        "\n",
        "A single train-test split gives **one estimate** of performance — which depends on which 20% of records happened to end up in the test set. **5-Fold Cross-Validation** gives a more robust estimate by training and evaluating the model across **5 different, non-overlapping partitions** of the training data.\n",
        "\n",
        "The resulting 5 F1-Scores tell us:\n",
        "* The **mean** — our best unbiased estimate of the model's true performance\n",
        "* The **standard deviation** — how consistent and stable the model is\n",
        "\n",
        "> **Critical:** Cross-validation is performed entirely **within `X_train_resampled`**. The held-out test set is never touched during this step.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "s3KuTtvb2JB2",
        "outputId": "739f0287-fdad-4049-ddb5-851105bee257"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "5-Fold Cross-Validation Results — Tuned Random Forest (F1-Score):\n",
            "────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
            "  Fold 1: 0.5993\n",
            "  Fold 2: 0.7538\n",
            "  Fold 3: 0.8651\n",
            "  Fold 4: 0.8731\n",
            "  Fold 5: 0.8694\n",
            "────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
            "  Mean F1-Score : 0.7921\n",
            "  Std Deviation : 0.1063\n",
            "  Min           : 0.5993\n",
            "  Max           : 0.8731\n",
            "\n",
            "✓ Cross-validation complete.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── 5-fold cross-validation on the tuned Random Forest ───────────────────────\n",
        "# Performed exclusively on the SMOTE-resampled training data\n",
        "cv_scores = cross_val_score(\n",
        "    best_rf_model,\n",
        "    X_train_resampled,\n",
        "    y_train_resampled,\n",
        "    cv      = 5,        # 5 non-overlapping folds\n",
        "    scoring = 'f1',     # F1-Score on each fold\n",
        "    n_jobs  = -1        # parallelise across all CPU cores\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"5-Fold Cross-Validation Results — Tuned Random Forest (F1-Score):\")\n",
        "print('─' * 48)\n",
        "for i, score in enumerate(cv_scores, 1):\n",
        "    print(f\"  Fold {i}: {score:.4f}\")\n",
        "print('─' * 48)\n",
        "print(f\"  Mean F1-Score : {cv_scores.mean():.4f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Std Deviation : {cv_scores.std():.4f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Min           : {cv_scores.min():.4f}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Max           : {cv_scores.max():.4f}\")\n",
        "print(\"\\n✓ Cross-validation complete.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "Ri3M1kI-FkWx"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Insights from cross-validation — exact scores from the output:**\n",
        "\n",
        "| Fold | F1-Score |\n",
        "|------|---------|\n",
        "| Fold 1 | **0.5993** |\n",
        "| Fold 2 | 0.7538 |\n",
        "| Fold 3 | 0.8651 |\n",
        "| Fold 4 | 0.8731 |\n",
        "| Fold 5 | 0.8694 |\n",
        "| **Mean** | **0.7921** |\n",
        "| **Std Dev** | **0.1063** |\n",
        "| Min | 0.5993 |\n",
        "| Max | 0.8731 |\n",
        "\n",
        "**Key observations:**\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Mean F1-Score of 0.7921** is identical to the best GridSearchCV cross-validated F1-Score — this is expected since GridSearchCV uses the same 5-fold CV internally. It confirms the GridSearchCV result was not a lucky single split.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Standard Deviation of 0.1063 is relatively high.** The range from Fold 1 (0.5993) to Fold 4 (0.8731) spans **0.274** — the model's performance varies significantly depending on which partition of the training data it sees.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Fold 1 (0.5993) is a clear outlier.** Folds 2–5 are consistently 0.75–0.87, but Fold 1 drops to 0.60. This suggests the first fold may contain a disproportionate share of harder-to-classify minority samples, or a different distribution of `building_dimension` values. The underlying cause is the synthetic nature of SMOTE samples — some folds will contain more synthetic vs real minority records, affecting consistency.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Practical interpretation:** The tuned Random Forest is expected to achieve approximately **0.79 F1-Score** on the training distribution. Given the test set retains real-world imbalance, the test F1-Score will be lower — which is normal and expected. The cross-validation score is an estimate of in-distribution performance, not out-of-sample performance."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "H7V7Ezpq2JB3"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "## 5. Feature Importance Analysis and Model Interpretability\n",
        "\n",
        "Understanding *why* the model makes its predictions is as important as the predictions themselves — especially in a business context where underwriters need to justify risk decisions.\n",
        "\n",
        "We use two complementary approaches:\n",
        "1. **Random Forest Feature Importance (Gini)** — which features reduce prediction uncertainty the most across all trees?\n",
        "2. **SHAP Values** — how much does each feature contribute to each individual prediction, and in which direction?\n",
        "\n",
        "Feature importance tells us *which* features matter globally. SHAP tells us *how* and *in which direction* each feature influences each specific prediction.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "GbnQ2j712JB4"
      },
      "source": [
        "### a. Random Forest Built-in Feature Importance (Gini Impurity Reduction)\n",
        "\n",
        "Random Forest measures feature importance by computing how much each feature **reduces the Gini impurity** (a measure of classification uncertainty) when used as a split. Features that consistently produce cleaner splits across many trees receive higher importance scores.\n",
        "\n",
        "**Limitation:** This metric tells us *which* features matter and *how much* — but not *in which direction* they push predictions. SHAP will fill this gap.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/",
          "height": 380
        },

              "\n",
              "      async function convertToInteractive(key) {\n",
              "        const element = document.querySelector('#df-cb09a84a-af4d-43b1-9eb0-9a8b46734d7f');\n",
              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",
              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",
              "  </div>\n",
              "\n",
              "\n",
              "    </div>\n",
              "  </div>\n"
            ],
            "application/vnd.google.colaboratory.intrinsic+json": {
              "type": "dataframe",
              "summary": "{\n  \"name\": \"display(importance_df\",\n  \"rows\": 10,\n  \"fields\": [\n    {\n      \"column\": \"Feature\",\n      \"properties\": {\n        \"dtype\": \"string\",\n        \"num_unique_values\": 10,\n        \"samples\": [\n          \"building_painted_V\",\n          \"building_dimension\",\n          \"transformed_insured_period\"\n        ],\n        \"semantic_type\": \"\",\n        \"description\": \"\"\n      }\n    },\n    {\n      \"column\": \"Importance\",\n      \"properties\": {\n        \"dtype\": \"number\",\n        \"std\": 0.07279777667501344,\n        \"min\": 0.02092857360762728,\n        \"max\": 0.2164424715230636,\n        \"num_unique_values\": 10,\n        \"samples\": [\n          0.02647440342694829,\n          0.21312338315172602,\n          0.05353250478167866\n        ],\n        \"semantic_type\": \"\",\n        \"description\": \"\"\n      }\n    }\n  ]\n}"
            }
          },
          "metadata": {}
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Extract feature importances from the tuned Random Forest ──────────────────\n",
        "importances   = best_rf_model.feature_importances_\n",
        "feature_names = X_train_resampled.columns\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Build a sorted importance DataFrame ───────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "importance_df = (\n",
        "    pd.DataFrame({'Feature': feature_names, 'Importance': importances})\n",
        "    .sort_values('Importance', ascending=False)\n",
        "    .reset_index(drop=True)\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"Top 10 Most Important Features (by Gini Impurity Reduction):\")\n",
        "display(importance_df.head(10))"
      ]

        "| 3 | `log_building_age` | **0.1448** |\n",
        "| 4 | `building_age` | **0.1130** |\n",
        "| 5 | `number_of_windows` | **0.0822** |\n",
        "| 6 | `transformed_insured_period` | **0.0535** |\n",
        "| 7 | `building_type` | **0.0496** |\n",
        "| 8 | `insured_period` | **0.0492** |\n",
        "| 9 | `building_painted_V` | **0.0265** |\n",
        "| 10 | `residential` | **0.0209** |\n",
        "\n",
        "**Key observations:**\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Building size and age dominate:** Together, `log_building_dimension` + `building_dimension` account for **42.95%** of total feature importance; `log_building_age` + `building_age` account for a further **25.78%**. These two physical characteristics alone explain **68.73%** of the model's decision-making.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **Both original and transformed versions are used:** The model uses `log_building_dimension` (0.2164) and `building_dimension` (0.2131) almost equally — the log scale provides slightly more discriminatory power overall, but the linear scale remains highly informative. The same pattern holds for `building_age`.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **`number_of_windows` is the only other feature above 8%:** At 0.0822, it confirms EDA findings — the window count is the third most important physical characteristic.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **The urban/rural cluster (`building_fenced_V`, `garden_V`, `settlement_U`)** does not appear in the top 10 — consistent with the contingency analysis showing these three features are highly redundant. They collectively carry limited additional signal beyond each other.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **`building_painted_V` (0.0265) and `residential` (0.0209)** contribute modest but non-zero importance — they are retained in the model as useful minor discriminators."
      ]

      "cell_type": "markdown",

        "id": "fhKXnieI2JB6"
      },
      "source": [
        "### b. SHAP Analysis — Directional Feature Contributions\n",
        "\n",
        "SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) provides the most rigorous measure of feature importance. Unlike the Gini method, SHAP quantifies — for each individual prediction — **how much each feature pushed the prediction toward or away from a claim**, and in **which direction**.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "K7kZ3DlA2JB6",
        "outputId": "30abbe64-1f23-4e58-ecc6-367f3ddbfc19"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ SHAP imported successfully.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Install and import SHAP ───────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "!pip install shap -q\n",
        "\n",
        "import shap\n",
        "print(\"✓ SHAP imported successfully.\")"
      ]
    },

      "cell_type": "code",

      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "G2WJDqlS2JB7",
        "outputId": "688ae00a-871d-4a7a-f100-f5e6e4a93176"

      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ SHAP values computed on the test set.\n",
            "  Shape of SHAP values array (class 1): (1403, 13)\n",
            "  → 1403 buildings × 13 features\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Create a TreeExplainer for the tuned Random Forest ───────────────────────\n",
        "# TreeExplainer is optimised for tree-based models — fast and exact\n",
        "explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(best_rf_model)\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Compute SHAP values for the test set ─────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "# We explain the TEST SET (never training) to get honest, generalised explanations\n",
        "shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)\n",
        "\n",
        "# shap_values is a list: [shap_for_class_0, shap_for_class_1]\n",
        "# We focus on class 1 (Claim probability) for all SHAP visualisations\n",
        "if isinstance(shap_values, list):\n",
        "    shap_values_claim = shap_values[1]           # 2D output format\n",
        "else:\n",
        "    shap_values_claim = shap_values[:, :, 1]     # 3D output format\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"✓ SHAP values computed on the test set.\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Shape of SHAP values array (class 1): {shap_values_claim.shape}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  → {shap_values_claim.shape[0]} buildings × {shap_values_claim.shape[1]} features\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "-2i_KKIQGOv4"
      },
      "source": [
        "**SHAP Summary Plot (Beeswarm / Dot Plot)**\n",
        "\n",
        "This is the most information-dense SHAP visualisation. It shows — simultaneously — the **importance ranking** and the **directional impact** of each feature.\n",
        "\n",
        "**How to read this plot:**\n",
        "* Features are ranked **top to bottom** by mean absolute SHAP value (most important at top)\n",
        "* Each **dot** represents one prediction (one building in the test set)\n",
        "* **Colour** encodes the feature value: 🔴 Red = high value, 🔵 Blue = low value\n",
        "* **Horizontal position** encodes the SHAP value: right = pushes prediction toward Claim (1), left = pushes toward No Claim (0)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": null,
      "metadata": {

        "plt.figure()\n",

        "    shap_values_claim, X_test,\n",
        "    feature_names = X_test.columns.tolist(),\n",
        "    plot_type     = 'dot',    # shows importance + direction + magnitude simultaneously\n",

        "**SHAP Waterfall Plot — Explaining One Individual Prediction**\n",
        "\n",
        "The waterfall plot explains **one specific building's prediction** in complete detail — answering: *Why did the model predict this particular building as high or low risk?*\n",
        "\n",
        "This is the most powerful tool for business communication — an underwriter can use this chart to justify a premium decision for a specific policy to a client or regulator.\n"
      ]

          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── SHAP Waterfall Plot for a single building ────────────────────────────────\n",
        "# Change sample_index to inspect the explanation for any other building in the test set\n",
        "sample_index = 0\n",
        "\n",
        "explanation = shap.Explanation(\n",
        "    values        = shap_values_claim[sample_index],    # SHAP values for this building\n",
        "    base_values   = explainer.expected_value[1],         # model's baseline expected value\n",
        "    data          = X_test.iloc[sample_index].values,    # actual feature values\n",
        "    feature_names = X_test.columns.tolist()\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "shap.plots.waterfall(explanation, show=False)\n",
        "plt.title(f'SHAP Waterfall Plot — Building Index {sample_index} in Test Set',\n",
        "          fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')\n",
        "plt.tight_layout()\n",
        "plt.savefig('shap_waterfall.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n",
        "plt.show()\n",
        "print(f\"✓ Waterfall plot saved for test set building index {sample_index}.\")"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "SAeoGhSAGu3i"
      },
      "source": [
        "**What the SHAP analysis confirms — aligning with and extending the EDA and feature importance findings:**\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **`log_building_dimension` and `building_dimension` are the dominant drivers** of individual claim predictions (SHAP summary: both at top of the beeswarm plot). High building dimension values (red dots) push strongly to the right — toward Claim (1). This directional confirmation was not available from feature importance alone, and is consistent with the EDA finding that claim buildings have a median dimension of 1,995.5 m² vs 900.0 m² for non-claim buildings.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **`log_building_age` and `building_age` show the same directional signal:** High age values (red dots) produce positive SHAP values — pushing predictions toward Claim. Older buildings are riskier. This aligns with the grouped statistics (claim buildings have higher mean `building_age`) and the physical logic of accumulated wear.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **`number_of_windows` is consistently positive:** More windows → higher SHAP values → higher claim probability. Consistent with the grouped statistics showing claim buildings have a median of 3.0 windows vs 1.0 for non-claim buildings.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **The SHAP dependence plot for `log_building_dimension`** (the top feature) shows that claim probability increases non-linearly with building dimension, with the steepest increase occurring in the medium-to-large size range. The interaction feature (auto-selected) reveals which secondary feature modulates this relationship.\n",
        "\n",
        "*   **The waterfall plot for Building 0 in the test set** shows the specific combination of feature values that drove its prediction — a low-risk prediction in this case, with small building dimension and low building age pushing the prediction well below the base rate of ~0.23."
      ]
    },

        "\n",
